retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandible -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: A bifid or trifid inferior alveolar canal may be present in rare instances. This can be detected on X-ray as a second or third mandibular canal. Branches of the inferior alveolar nerve commonly run through these extra foramina and can confer a risk for inadequate anesthesia during surgical procedures involving the mandible. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the mandible, central and lateral incisors have single roots, with central incisors often lacking apical curvature. [6] If present in either tooth, apical curvature tends to be distally inclined. [6] Mandibular canines usually present with single roots, but one unique difference between maxillary and mandibular canines is that mandibular canines may have bifurcated roots similar to those seen in maxillary first premolars. [6] The mandibular first and second premolars typically present with single roots but may have bifurcated roots in rare situations. The root tips are often inclined distally, and the mandibular second premolar's root is usually longer than the first premolar's. [7] The mandibular first and second molars usually have two distally tilted roots: a mesial and a distal root. The mandibular second molar's roots are usually shorter with less splay than the first molar roots.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, the mandibular third molars typically have two shorter, often parallel, mesial, and distal roots with a more pronounced taper than the first or second mandibular molars. [8] Like the maxillary third molar, the mandibular third molar can have significant morphological variation. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Mandibular Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Incisors - The incisors of the mandible are smaller than the incisors of the maxillary teeth and have one root that contains a single root canal. The central incisors constitute teeth P and O. The lateral incisors constitute teeth Q and N. The root of the incisors are slightly flattened. The crown is trapezoidal in the labial view and contains three tubercled incisal edges. Canines – The canines constitute teeth R and M. Their crown is trapezoidal with a single labial cusp. A single root (the longest of the teeth) contains a single root canal. Mandibular molars : The buccal, lingual, mesial, and distal surfaces of the mandibular molars are all convex. The occlusal surface is rectangular.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of one or more of these radiographic features indicates the need for further imaging by way of CBCT, which can provide further information on the relationship between the lower third molar and the IAN.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Foramen -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: The shape of the mandibular foramen may vary depending on its contents. The mandibular foramen's location, thickness, and length are dependent on the inferior alveolar nerve and artery. [2] [3] The shape of the orifice of the mandibular foramen can be circular or even V-shaped in some individuals. The mandibular foramen vary depending on the age of the person also. The opening of the mandibular foramen is generally more superior in the ramus of the mandible in adults. In contrast, the mandibular foramen is located lower in the mandible in children. [4] In some cases, the mandibular foramen was found to have an extra canal. The extra foramen is called the accessory mandibular foramen. This extra foramen is present in individuals with bifid inferior alveolar nerves. The location of the accessory mandibular foramen is inferior to the primary mandibular foramen. In rarer variations, there are three mental foramina as the endpoint of the mandibular foramen. [5] [6] These variations can be found and confirmed on radiographic imaging. [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Mandibular Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Second Molars – The mandibular second molars(tooth T, K) morphologically resemble the first mandibular molar of the permanent teeth. Its buccal surface is divided into the mesiobuccal, distobuccal, and distal cusps. The lingual cusps include the distolingual and the mesiolingual cusps. Numerous differences exist between the primary and permanent teeth, for example: Primary teeth are smaller Crowding is uncommon in primary teeth due to adequate space Primary teeth are whiter Pulp chambers of primary teeth are larger The enamel and dentin of primary teeth are thinner The area of contact of the primary molars are flat and broad The roots of the primary molar teeth are narrow and slender Pulp size relative to the crown is larger in primary teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Foramen -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The mandibular foramen is a bony canal formed within the mandible. This bony canal opens on the inner side of the mandibular ramus. The opening is directly inferior to the mandibular notch and ends at the mental foramen. The foramen follows the shape of the mandible. The canal starts inferior to the mandibular notch and descends until it's slightly superior to the angle of the mandible. At the point superior to the angle of the mandible, the canal will make a turn towards the anterior chin region. After the canal makes the turn, it will travel and end at the mental foramen, which is slightly lateral to the anterior chin region. The mandibular foramen contains a branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V), a branch of the maxillary artery and the inferior alveolar vein. The nerve and blood vessels within the mandibular foramen are the inferior alveolar nerve, artery, and vein. The inferior alveolar nerve is responsible for innervation of the gums, dental sockets, and mandible. The inferior alveolar artery is the dominant blood supply to the mandible, gums, teeth, and nerves in the lower jaw. The inferior alveolar vein is responsible for the venous drainage of the mandible and tissues that are nearby.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth agenesis may involve the primary or permanent dentition and is a developmental anomaly where at least one tooth is absent. It can be further classified as hypodontia, involving 1 to 5 absent teeth, and oligodontia involving six or more missing teeth. When excluding third molars (wisdom teeth), tooth agenesis has a prevalence between 3% and 10% in the permanent dentition. Third molars are the most commonly congenitally missing permanent teeth, with a reported prevalence of 23%. This is followed by mandibular second premolars, maxillary lateral incisors, and, less frequently, the maxillary second premolars. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Extraction of Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative CBCT before tooth extraction is mainly related to impacted mandibular third molars due to the risk of injuring the inferior alveolar nerve (IAN) during surgery. [8] However, the radiograph of choice before removing mandibular third molars is orthopantomography. This x-ray will provide information about the proximity of the tooth with the IAN. The most common radiographic signs associated with IAN injury are the darkening of the roots of the third molar, interruption of the cortical lines of the canal, and diversion of the canal. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Loss of cortication of the inferior dental canal (IDC) - this is seen as an absence of the white 'tram lines' on plain film radiographs. Darkening of third molar roots - this is seen as a dark 'banding' across the apical area of the lower third molar roots. Deviation of the IDC - the IDC typically deviates away from the roots of the lower third molar. Narrowing of the IDC - this is characterized by localized narrowing of the IDC around the region of the lower third molar root. Dilaceration of roots - This is classified as excess curvature of the lower third molar roots. Narrowing of the third molar roots - This is seen as an abnormal narrowing of the apical area of the lower third molar roots.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Reconstruction -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mental foramen is found inferior to the first pre-molar on each side. The digastric and mylohyoid muscles attach to the inner, lingual cortex of the mandible. The mandible supports sixteen adult teeth that, ideally, occlude against sixteen maxillary teeth. [4] A branch of the mandibular portion of the maxillary artery, the inferior alveolar artery, is the mandible's primary blood supply and runs through the mandibular canal. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Accessory canals can be present in different numbers and are located laterally. In odontogenesis, lateral accessory canals form when the root sheath encounters a blood vessel. Two types of nerve fibers are normally present in the root: myelinated nerves (the minority) and unmyelinated nerves (the majority). The nerves are of trigeminal origin, from the mandibular and maxillary branches of the trigeminal nerve. The cell bodies are in the trigeminal ganglion and the odontoblastic layer of the pulp, near the odontoblasts.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: When divided on the mid-line sagittal plane, these teeth are organized into the right and left halves. Incisors and canines can be classified further as "anterior teeth" and molars and premolars as "posterior teeth." The primary teeth erupt during the first year and into the third years of life and are then exfoliated(shed) between the ages of 6 and 12. Since primary teeth are shed, as some trees shed their leaves, they are also referred to as deciduous teeth, which are eventually replaced by the adult permanent dentition. [2] Primary teeth are labeled in the following classification system and are designated in a clockwise direction: A: Right maxillary 2nd molar B: Right maxillary 1st molar C: Right maxillary canine D: Right maxillary lateral incisor E: Right maxillary central incisor F: Left maxillary central incisor G: Left maxillary lateral incisor H: Left maxillary canine I: Left maxillary 1st molar J: Left maxillary 2nd molar T: Right mandibular 2nd molar

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: An additional cusp called the cusp of Carabelli is sometimes located on the mesio-palatal surface of permanent maxillary first molars and is rarely present on the other permanent molars. Although less common, an accessory cusp can protrude from the cingulum of the primary or permanent anterior teeth, referred to as the talon cusp. [26] Macrodontia and microdontia are rare shape anomalies of dentition, where teeth are larger or smaller than average. [27] [28] Taurodontism is an abnormality of tooth morphology that often occurs in multi-rooted teeth and is characterized by a short root and an enlarged body containing an equally enlarged pulp chamber. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: The cervical canal structure is anatomically situated within the structure of the internal os, with the internal os serving as the parent structure that encompasses the cervical canal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1906
retrieved_docs.content: The head of the first metatarsal bone is a distinct anatomical region of the foot that is defined by laterality, indicating that it can be identified as belonging to either the left or right side.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1904
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on ear are classified as a subcategory within the broader group of diagnostické techniky otologické.
